source_text.text:
[PDF_PAGE 68 | score=5] ! I i Sylvan Cream. Elves, satyrs, dryads, tre- ants, sylphs, and similar native creatures rep- resent a priceless resouTce every druid should cultivate to the utmost. Their special powers and ability to blend into the wilderness make them excellent scouts. In addition, their goals usually parallel those of the druid-the pro- tection of the woodlands and wilderness-so their iriformation likely will prove timely and reliable. A character tends to receive a more regula flow of gossip by befriending purticu- lar sylvan beings-make friends with Shay- lara the Pixie rather than just staying on good terms with a ”band of pixies.” To foster this comradeship, the druid should often visit the dwellin
[PDF_PAGE 46 | score=4] normally live in stone, wood, or mud-brick cottages and act as the protectors of a small tract of wilderness-a wood or river valley- or of a village or group of hamlets. Most druids of this status have the Village Druid or Guardian kit, and those who choose to pro- tect a village usually have become respected community leaders. Initiates between 7th and 11th level have received all their branch’s granted powers. Such druids live simply but have widened their areas of influence, perhaps becoming the guardians of entire forests or mountains, or of all the villages in a barony. These druids often dwell near a sacred grove surrounded by a few acres of virgin wilderness-a sanctu- ary for rare and
[PDF_PAGE 43 | score=3] I 1- 3 ~ With a Wanderer guide, a party can increase travel time by onesixth; thus, an unencum- bered party led by a Wanderer would travel 28 miles in a day, not 24. 1 Special Hindrances: Constantly on the move, a Wanderer never allows herself to be burdened. Fife cannot have retainers, hire- lhgs, mercenaries, or even servants una she Ireaches 12th level (but animal companions pan travel with her). The druid cannot pos- lsess more treasure than she can carry; she lpither converts the excess into a portable form (gems, etc.) or donates it to a worthy cause, The DM sets up an adventure in the Avenger chances upon a village ened by marauding monsters. After saving the villagers, he decides to 
[PDF_PAGE 64 | score=3] in any direction, DMs should set their cam- paigns at times when evil has grown alarm- ingly in strength, for such periods allow druid PCs to become heroes. With the balance threatened by the power of evil, most druids find themselves in the position to serve the cause of good willingly. This motivation is good for the game, as it provides plenty of adventure for good-aligned PCs while giving druids a chance to fight by their sides. Similarly, in a game featuring the PCs as rebels or outlaws against authority, the bal- ance may have swung in favor of inflexible law. In such a case, druids may side with the forces of chaos-good, neutral, and maybe even evil-in the struggle of liberty against 
[PDF_PAGE 65 | score=3] A druid also will oppose farming that re- quires the destruction of ancient wilderness areas, large spreads of land, or any region that serves as a sanctuary for rare, endan- gered, or magical plants and animals. Most importantly, no druid ever tolerates the de- struction of a sacred grove! Druids also dis- courage farming practices that harm the soil and selfish irrigation schemes (dams, aque- ducts, etc.) intended to parch one region to slake another’s thirst. Druids sometimes tolerate logging or clear- ing land to mine useful minerals like salt, copper, tin, and iron. Mer all, they use man- ufactured items themselves. Whether to accept such land use depends on the motives and need of thos
[PDF_PAGE 13 | score=2] do has @e following granted powers: Redeives a +2 bonus to all saving throw. vs. cold based attacks. Le a, the languages of intelligent mon- sters wpose natural habitats are tundra, arc- tic, an4 subarctic regions. The arctic druid gains o$e extra proficiency slot for this pur- pose ev#ry three levels (at 3rd, 6th, etc.). Such languages include those spoken by ettins, frost gibnts, ice toads, selkies, werebears, .white dkagons, winter wolves, verbeeg, yeti, and oth+rs. Ignbres the effects of freezing weather upon lu+nself at 3rd level. Ideptifies with perfect accuracy arctic d animals, thin ice (ice that would under the weight of a person or a re water at 3rd level. mal, marine mammal, or bi
[PDF_PAGE 22 | score=2] Fighbleddruids. Often acting as wandering guardidns of Nature and country folk (much like neqtral rangers), fighter/druids also can love Nature, and decide to adopt a ne become hermit-knighis, living away from society and defending a particular grove with their lives. Fighters who become druids often do so because they seek spiritual growth, because they have grown disgusted with the world of man, or occasionally as penance for a particular misdeed. Druids who become fighters, on the other hand, want to take a more direct approach to defending the wilderness; others seek to attune themselves to Nature by mastering their own bodies using eastern-style fighting arts, often becoming rather enig
[PDF_PAGE 25 | score=2] Druid Kits -7 .action Bonuses and Penatties p. 18) is expressed as a bonus (+l, +2, etc.) or a penalty (-1, -2, etc.). When rolling 2d10 for encounter reactions (Table 59, DMG, p. 103), the DM must subtract the bonus or add the penalty-not the other way around. For instance, the druid Snap- dragon has a combined +7 reaction adjust- ment bonus for her high Charisma and her druid kit. The DM then subtracts 7 from the 2d10 encounter reaction roll to reflect the bonus, due to the way Table 59 in the DMG is designed. Kib and the Character Record Sheet To record a druid kit on your character record sheet (pgs. 125-127), take the follow- Using A druid kit works with the basic PH druid or with #ny o
